elements.sent-2411-001: Stochastic processes model various natural phenomena from disease transmission to stock prices, but simulating and quantifying their uncertainty can be computationally challenging.
elements.sent-2411-002: For example, modeling a Gaussian Process with standard statistical methods incurs an $\mathcal{O (n^3)$ penalty, and even using state-of-the-art Neural Processes (NPs) incurs an $\mathcal{O (n^2)$ penalty due to the attention mechanism.}}
elements.sent-2411-003: We introduce the Transformer Neural Process - Kernel Regression (TNP-KR), a new architecture that incorporates a novel transformer block we call a Kernel Regression Block (KRBlock), which reduces the computational complexity of attention in transformer-based Neural Processes (TNPs) from $\mathcal{O ((n_C+n_T)^2)$ to $O(n_C^2+n_Cn_T)$ by eliminating masked computations, where $n_C$ is the number of context, and $n_T$ is the number of test points, respectively, and a fast attention variant that further reduces all attention calculations to $\mathcal{O (n_C)$ in space and time complexity.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: In benchmarks spanning such tasks as meta-regression, Bayesian optimization, and image completion, we demonstrate that the full variant matches the performance of state-of-the-art methods while training faster and scaling two orders of magnitude higher in number of test points, and the fast variant nearly matches that performance while scaling to millions of both test and context points on consumer hardware.
elements.sent-2411-005: The principle challenge of modern spatiotemporal Bayesian modeling is scale.
elements.sent-2411-006: As the number of observed locations increases from tens to thousands or hundreds of thousands, traditional techniques used to model spatiotemporal phenomena break down.
elements.sent-2411-007: Perhaps the most common method typically employed to model spatiotemporal processes is the Gaussian Process (GP).
elements.sent-2411-008: Gaussian Processes are a particularly well-behaved class of stochastic processes.
elements.sent-2411-009: Specifically, for a finite index set $\{t\in T\}$, the collection $X=(X_1,\ldots,X_T)$ follows a multivariate Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-010: This makes various analytic calculations tractable, facilitating regression, marginalization, and sampling with GPs.
elements.sent-2411-011: While GPs provide a significant degree of flexibility in modeling, the analytic solutions they yield do not scale well in the number of observed locations.
elements.sent-2411-012: Using a GP to model spatial random effects within a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler incurs an $\mathcal{O (n^3)$ cost per sample, where $n$ is the number of observed locations.}
elements.sent-2411-013: This is because the covariance matrix must be inverted, or factorized in the case of Cholesky decomposition, at each iteration in order to generate a sample.
elements.sent-2411-014: Unfortunately, this means that for only $n$ = 1,000 locations, nearly a billion operations must be performed to generate a single sample.
elements.sent-2411-015: In order to accelerate Bayesian inference with spatiotemporal stochastic processes, there have been at least three prominent strains of research.
elements.sent-2411-016: The first is Variational Inference (VI), which aims to recast the inference problem as an optimization problem and maximize the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO).
elements.sent-2411-017: The second aims to accelerate sampling by using a generative neural network-based approximation.
elements.sent-2411-018: This family tends to leverage Variational Autoencoders (VAEs).
elements.sent-2411-019: The third is a recent family of deep learning models called Neural Processes (NPs).
elements.sent-2411-020: These models use a meta-learning objective, meaning that once trained, the forward pass of the model takes as input ``context'' or observed points and returns a function.
elements.sent-2411-021: This function can then be evaluated at any collection of test points and returns both their mean predictions and associated uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-022: The NP family of models has grown rapidly over the last few years, but recently Transformer Neural Processes (TNPs), including TNP-D, TNP-ND, and TNP-A have come to dominate the landscape \citep{tnp .}
elements.sent-2411-023: These models, however, suffer from an $\mathcal{O (n^2)$ complexity due to the attention mechanism used in transformer encoder blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-024: We extend this family with the Transformer Neural Process - Kernel Regression (TNP-KR) model.
elements.sent-2411-025: TNP-KR uses a novel transformer block we call a Kernel Regression Block (KRBlock), which reduces the cost of attention in transformer-based NPs from $\mathcal{O ((n_C+n_T)^2)$ to $\mathcal{O (n_C^2+n_cn_T)$ where $n_C$ is the number of context points and $n_T$ is the number of test points.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: We also introduce an even faster variant, which uses Performer attention inside the KRBlock \citep{performer .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Performer attention uses a kernel approximation to softmax attention and further reduces the complexity to $\mathcal{O (n_C)$, enabling the model to scale to millions of points on consumer hardware.}
elements.sent-2411-028: We show that the full variant matches the performance of state-of-the-art methods while training faster and scaling two orders of magnitude higher in number of test points, and the fast variant nearly matches that performance while scaling to millions of both test and context points on consumer hardware.
elements.sent-2411-029: A number of general techniques have been developed to reduce the computational burden of modeling large spatiotemporal datasets.
elements.sent-2411-030: These include, but are not limited to variational inference (VI) \citep{vi_review , stochastic process emulation \citep{pi_vae,prior_vae,prior_cvae , and neural processes (NPs) \citep{np,cnp,anp,bnp,convcnp,tnp .}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: There is also a long literature on approximate methods to scale up Gaussian processes in particular which we do not cover in detail, see e.g.~\citet{hensman2013gaussian, rue2009approximate, solin2020hilbert, wilson2015thoughts, lindgren2010explicit .}
elements.sent-2411-032: While using distinct algorithmic approaches, all these methods provide approximations to the posterior distributions.
elements.sent-2411-033: VI \citep{vi_review, pml2 approximates the posterior distribution by framing inference as an optimization problem, aiming to maximize the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) by minimizing the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between a variational distribution $q_\psi(\mathbf{z )$ and the true posterior $p_\theta(\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{x )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Although VI is widely used, its effectiveness depends on selecting an appropriate variational family, and there are no guarantees on how close the ELBO is to the true log-likelihood, making uncertainty estimation challenging when the variational family poorly approximates the true distribution~\citep{yao2018yes, huggins2020validated .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Another line of research aims to accelerate sampling by approximating samples from computationally intensive stochastic processes.
elements.sent-2411-036: This is the aim of models like PriorVAE, PriorCVAE, and $\pi$VAE \citep{prior_vae,prior_cvae,pi_vae .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Currently these models are all based on Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) \citep{vae .}
elements.sent-2411-038: VAEs consist of an encoder and decoder combined with a latent sampling process.
elements.sent-2411-039: They encode raw data into a vector of latent parameters, which are then used to sample a latent vector.
elements.sent-2411-040: This latent vector is then passed through the decoder, whose objective is to recreate the original data.
elements.sent-2411-041: The advantage of models like these is that if the latent distribution is simple, i.e.~a multivariate normal with diagonal covariance, it can be very easy to sample.
elements.sent-2411-042: This means that a fully trained network can generate new samples from the original data distribution by sampling latents and passing them through the decoder.
elements.sent-2411-043: Furthermore, this can often be done in time linear in the number of layers in the network, which can be two orders of magnitude faster than sampling from a real GP \citep{prior_cvae .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Because neural networks are inherently differentiable, they can also be transparently integrated into inference frameworks like NumPyro, where gradient-informed samplers like the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS) can easily pass gradients through the model.
elements.sent-2411-045: The principle challenge with this class of models is that the number of input and output locations is fixed and ordered, which means a new model must be retrained each time the number of observed locations changes or the location associated with each input changes.
elements.sent-2411-046: These models are also sensitive to the dimensionality of the latent vector, which induces an information bottleneck on autoencoded data and can cause oversmoothing in generated samples.
elements.sent-2411-047: Neural Processes (NPs) are a family of models that use deep neural networks to represent stochastic processes.
elements.sent-2411-048: NPs are considered ``meta-learners'' because, instead of modelling a single function $h: \mathbf{s \to \mathbf{f}$, they take as input context points $(\mathbf{s _C, \mathbf{f _C)$ and return a distribution over functions $g: (\mathbf{s _C,\mathbf{f _C) \to P_{h \mid \mathbf{s _C, \mathbf{f _C (h: \mathbf{s _T \to \mathbf{f _T)$, allowing evaluation at test points $\mathbf{s _T$ without retraining.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: To be valid stochastic processes, NPs must ensure invariance to context/test point ordering and consistency under marginalization.
elements.sent-2411-050: There are two main classes of Neural Processes, latent neural processes and conditional neural processes.
elements.sent-2411-051: Latent neural processes dedicate part of their architecture to generating latent parameters, which are sampled and passed through the decoder to generate coherent samples, similar to VAEs.
elements.sent-2411-052: The fundamental assumption of latent NPs is that the test points are independent conditional on the latent vector.
elements.sent-2411-053: For instance, if $(\mathbf{s _C,\mathbf{f _C)$ represents a tuple of locations and function values at context (observed) points, $(\mathbf{s _T,\mathbf{f _T)$ represents locations and function values at test (unobserved) points, and $\mathbf{z}$ represents a sampled latent vector, the likelihood of the function values at test locations can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{latent} \begin{split} p(\mathbf{f}_T\mid\mathbf{s}_T,\mathbf{s}_C,\mathbf{f}_C) =\int p(\mathbf{z}\mid\mathbf{s}_C,\mathbf{f}_C)p(\mathbf{f}_T\mid\mathbf{s}_T,\mathbf{z})d\mathbf{z} =\int p(\mathbf{z}\mid\mathbf{s}_C,\mathbf{f}_C)\prod_{i=1}^{|T|} p(\mathbf{f}_T^{(i)}\mid\mathbf{s}_T^{(i)},\mathbf{z})d\mathbf{z} \end{split}\end{equation} Conditional neural processes often have very similar architectures, except they avoid sampling a latent vector and condition on a fixed representation, $\mathbf{r}$, of the context points.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: This implies the following factorization, assuming the encoder has already processed the context points, $\text{enc (\mathbf{s _C,\mathbf{f _C)=\mathbf{r}$: \begin{equation}\label{conditional} \begin{split} p(\mathbf{f}_T\mid\mathbf{s}_T,\mathbf{s}_C,\mathbf{f}_C) =\prod_{i=1}^{|T|} p(\mathbf{f}_T^{(i)}\mid\mathbf{s}_T^{(i)}, \mathbf{r}) \end{split}\end{equation} In practice, conditional neural processes tend to perform better.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: It is unclear whether this is because the latent vectors, $\mathbf{z}$, are an insufficient representation of the latent parameters, or if the models are expending some of their finite capacity on producing good estimates of $\mathbf{z}$ at the cost of final predictive accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-056: Either way, the most performant NPs as measured by log-likelihood scores are conditional, specifically conditional transformer neural processes (TNPs).
elements.sent-2411-057: The advantage of NPs is that they can be pretrained across a variety of priors and number of context points.
elements.sent-2411-058: And at test time, they can perform GP regression for thousands of paths simultaneously in seconds on a GPU.
elements.sent-2411-059: This can be several orders of magnitude faster than calculating uncertainty for each path within an inference framework.
elements.sent-2411-060: The disadvantage of these networks is that previously they significantly underfit relative to true GPs, particularly at context or observed data points.
elements.sent-2411-061: However, NPs have evolved from simple MLP-based networks to sophisticated convolutional and transformer-based architectures.
elements.sent-2411-062: These newer variants are often just as accurate as baseline GPs and at least an order of magnitude faster.
elements.sent-2411-063: We detail the essential aspects of modern canonical designs in this section.
elements.sent-2411-064: For brevity, we omit the original NP \citep{np and CNP \citep{cnp models, which used an autoencoder framework with MLPs.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Also relevant, but not included here are Bootstrapping Neural Processes \citep{bnp , which represent an extension to all NP models.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The Attentive Neural Process (ANP) was introduced to address several limitations of the NP and CNP architectures \citep{anp .}
elements.sent-2411-067: In particular, NPs and CNPs underfit on observed context points and generally tend to oversmooth the posterior predictive.
elements.sent-2411-068: One of the principle reasons for underfitting is that the decoder is unable to differentially attend to each of the context points when decoding at test points.
elements.sent-2411-069: For instance, if one context point is located at $\mathbf{s _c=-2$ and the test point is at $\mathbf{s _t=2$, i.e.~opposite ends of the training region, $\mathbf{s _c$ equally influences the context vector used to decode at $\mathbf{s _t$ despite having little to no influence on the behavior of function at $\mathbf{s _t=2$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Accordingly, the authors propose an attentive version of the NP that allows both local and global context to be incorporated when decoding at test locations.
elements.sent-2411-071: The ANP shares the NP architecture, but replaces MLPs with multihead dot product self-attention in both the latent and deterministic encoding paths.
elements.sent-2411-072: Then, in the deterministic path it adds cross-attention between test points and context points so that the associated context vector, $\mathbf{r _t$, summarizes information relevant to the test location $\mathbf{s _t$, rather than being a global representation.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: This localized context vector, $\mathbf{r _t$, is passed through the decoder with the global latent representation $\mathbf{z}$, and the location, $\mathbf{s _t$, to produce $(\mu_t,\sigma^2_t)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: With the ANP and its conditional variant CANP, the NP family started to become a viable replacement to true GPs for inference.
elements.sent-2411-075: Convolutional Conditional Neural Processes (ConvCNP) were designed to incorporate translation equivariance into NPs \citep{convcnp .}
elements.sent-2411-076: When a model exhibits translation equivariance, it is able to identify a feature or function behavior regardless of how far its input has shifted in the domain.
elements.sent-2411-077: This improves the model's capacity to generalize beyond the training region.
elements.sent-2411-078: The authors of ConvCNP define two architectures, one for on-the-grid data and one for off-the-grid data.
elements.sent-2411-079: Here we detail the off-the-grid version since it is more generic and can be used in both cases.
elements.sent-2411-080: First, the domain is partitioned into a fixed, uniform grid.
elements.sent-2411-081: Then, a positive-definite Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) kernel is evaluated at each of the grid points using the context set.
elements.sent-2411-082: This value is then normalized using a density channel so the point values are invariant to the cardinality of the context set.
elements.sent-2411-083: This grid is then run through a CNN-based architecture, e.g. ResNet \citep{ResNet , to create an updated hidden state representation at grid locations.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Finally, the decoder uses another RHKS kernel, typically the same one used in the encoder, to decode test points using the underlying hidden state grid values.
elements.sent-2411-085: ConvCNPs perform very well on the standard NP benchmarks, often exceeding the performance of other models at a fraction of the number of learnable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, beyond simple examples, ConvCNP requires many more parameters, often on the same order as other models, to perform competitively.
elements.sent-2411-087: Furthermore, ConvCNP is very sensitive to the parameterization of the intermediate grid and the effective receptive field of the CNN layer.
elements.sent-2411-088: For instance, at lower lengthscales, the model performs better when there is a higher grid resolution, but this increase in grid resolution changes the receptive field of the CNN layer, so the CNN's kernels must be optimized in conjunction.
elements.sent-2411-089: Lastly, due to the fixed intermediate grid, ConvCNP suffers from the curse of dimensionality and is difficult to evaluate in non-contiguous regions of the domain.
elements.sent-2411-090: Transformer Neural Processes (TNPs) \citep{tnp can be considered an extension of the Conditional Attentive Neural Process (CANP) that uses multiple transformer encoder blocks instead of stacked self-attention and cross-attention.}
elements.sent-2411-091: A transformer encoder block consists of a self-attention layer followed by a feedfoward network with residual connections interspersed.
elements.sent-2411-092: The pseudocode can be viewed in Appendix \ref{algorithms .}
elements.sent-2411-093: Because standard transformers are designed to work with sequences of data and use fixed positional embeddings, the TNP authors had to modify the architecture for NPs.
elements.sent-2411-094: Accordingly, TNPs dispense with the positional embeddings, merge the context and test sequences as input, and introduce a special attention mask.
elements.sent-2411-095: The context sequence consists of location and function value pairs, $[(\mathbf{s _1,\mathbf{f _1),\ldots,(\mathbf{s _{n_C ,\mathbf{f _{n_C )]$, and the test sequence consists of location and zero-padded function value pairs, $[(\mathbf{s _{1 ,\mathbf{0 ),\ldots,(\mathbf{s _{n_T ,\mathbf{0 )]$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Within each layer, a mask is applied that prevents context points from attending to test points and prevents test points from attending to other test points.
elements.sent-2411-097: This means that context points only attend to other context points and test points also only attend to context points.
elements.sent-2411-098: After the encoding stage, the embeddings for test points are passed through a prediction head that estimates the mean and covariance structures.
elements.sent-2411-099: There are three original TNP variants: TNP-D, TNP-ND, and TNP-A.
elements.sent-2411-100: TNP-D (Diagonal) assumes test points can be factorized independently conditional on the context points, in line with most CNP variants.
elements.sent-2411-101: TNP-ND (Non-Diagonal) parameterizes a covariance matrix by estimating the elements in the lower triangular matrix of a Cholesky decomposition for test points.
elements.sent-2411-102: Lastly, TNP-A (Autoregressive) assumes that the test points can be factorized autoregressively.
elements.sent-2411-103: This means that each time a test point is predicted, its true value is then added to the context set and used when predicting the next test point.
elements.sent-2411-104: In practice, we found TNP-D to consistently perform well.
elements.sent-2411-105: On the other hand, we found TNP-ND could become arbitrarily miscalibrated.
elements.sent-2411-106: When there are very few context points, TNP-ND maximizes the log-likelihood by collapsing the standard deviation for test points that lie near context points, which provides very precise estimates.
elements.sent-2411-107: However, this also causes the model to collapse uncertainty estimates for test points that lie far away from context points, leading to both high log-likelihoods and high miscalibration rates.
elements.sent-2411-108: On the other hand, TNP-A assumes that the true test points are observed autoregressively, which is equivalent to running any NP model forward one test point at a time.
elements.sent-2411-109: This method yields log-likelihoods that correspond to sequential observations, rather than joint distributions over all unobserved points.
elements.sent-2411-110: As we are interested in the general case, we do not include experiments on TNP-A.
elements.sent-2411-111: TNP-KR was inspired by TNP-D, which consistently performs well on NP benchmarks and does not make the same assumptions as TNP-A or suffer from miscalibration like TNP-ND.
elements.sent-2411-112: One of the principal limitations of all TNP variants, however, is the $O((n_C+n_T)^2)$ attention used by the encoder layers.
elements.sent-2411-113: Recall that $n_C$ is the number of context points and $n_T$ is the number of test points.
elements.sent-2411-114: This also is not strictly necessary because the special mask applied after attention discards many of the calculations, namely, those from context points to test points and those from test points to other test points.
elements.sent-2411-115: Accordingly, we introduce the KRBlock, a transformer block that avoids computing $\mathcal{O (n_T^2+n_CnT)$ attention values altogether.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Unlike the original transformer encoder block, TNP-KR also uses pre-normalized residual connections \citep{pre_norm , which has been shown to stabilize training and improve performance.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \label{architecture} \begin{center} \includegraphics[scale=0.75]{figures/KRBlock} \end{center} \caption{KRBlock architecture. Queries and keys have their own residual pathways, but keys are used to update queries through cross-attention.}
elements.sent-2411-117: We call this a KRBlock because the cross-attention from test to context points can be viewed as a form of Nadaraya-Watson kernel regression where the locations are the feature embeddings and the kernel is a dot product softmax kernel.
elements.sent-2411-118: Specifically, if $\alpha$ is the dot product softmax kernel, $q_i$ is a query embedding representing a test point, $k_j$ is a key embedding representing a context point, and $v_j$ is the value embedding associated with the same context point, cross-attention in the KRBlock can be formulated explicitly as Nadaraya-Watson kernel regression: \begin{equation}\label{cnp} \begin{split} f(\mathbf{q}_i)=\sum_j \frac{\alpha(\mathbf{q}_i,\mathbf{k}_j)}{\sum_j \alpha(\mathbf{q}_i,\mathbf{k}_j)}\mathbf{v}_j \end{split}\end{equation} Stacking KRBlocks then allows the model to perform iterative kernel regression on increasingly complex internal representations of test and context points.
elements.sent-2411-119: Cross-attention from test to context points costs $\mathcal{O (n_Cn_T)$ and the self-attention among contxt points costs $O(n_C^2)$, making the total complexity $O(n_C^2 + n_Cn_T)$.}
elements.sent-2411-120: When there are a large number of context points, the $\mathcal{O (n_C^2)$ term can still prove computationally prohibitive.}
elements.sent-2411-121: For example, in satellite imagery, a comparatively small image of 300x500 results in 150,000 locations or pixels \citep{heaton .}
elements.sent-2411-122: A common application is to inpaint pixels missing due to cloud cover.
elements.sent-2411-123: With 20\% of pixels missing, there would still be 120000 context points.
elements.sent-2411-124: This means that even with a KRBlock, the space and time complexity would be on the order of $120000^2$, requiring nearly 60GB of memory and 14.4 billion multiplications per self-attention application.
elements.sent-2411-125: Thus, in order to further scale KRBlocks, we incorporate Performer attention, also known as fast attention \citep{performer .}
elements.sent-2411-126: Fast attention is based on a complete algorithm called Fast Attention with Orthogonal Random features (FAVOR+).
elements.sent-2411-127: FAVOR+ allows attention to be calculated in linear space and time complexity without making any assumptions about the sparsity or rank of the attention matrix.
elements.sent-2411-128: It is nearly unbiased and offers uniform converenge and low variance \citep{performer .}
elements.sent-2411-129: FAVOR+ constructs an attention matrix $\mathbf{A ^{L\times L}$ where $\mathbf{A (i,j)=K(\mathbf{q _i,\mathbf{k _j)$ without ever fully materializing it.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: For a randomized mapping $\phi:\mathbb{R ^d\to\mathbb{R ^r_+$, entries can be estimated with $K(\mathbf{q ,\mathbf{k )=\mathbb{E}\left[\phi(\mathbf{q )^\intercal\phi(\mathbf{k )\right]$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: In fact, most kernels can be modeled using the following mapping: \begin{equation}\label{kernel_expansion} \begin{split} \phi(\mathbf{x})=\frac{\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})}{\sqrt{m}}\bigg[ f_1(\omega_1^\intercal\mathbf{x}),\ldots,f_1(\omega_m^\intercal\mathbf{x}),\ldots, f_l(\omega_1^\intercal\mathbf{x}), \ldots,f_l(\omega_m^\intercal\mathbf{x})\bigg] \end{split}\end{equation} where $f_1,\ldots,f_l: \mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $h:\mathbb{R ^d\to\mathbb{R}$, and $\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_m\stackrel{\text{iid {\sim}\mathcal{D}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: When $\mathcal{D =\mathcal{N (0,\mathbf{I _d)$, this leads to the Gaussian kernel, $K_\text{gauss}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Ignoring the $\sqrt{d}$ normalization, the softmax kernel is defined as $\operatorname{SM (\mathbf{q ,\mathbf{k )\coloneqq\exp(\mathbf{q ^\intercal\mathbf{k )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Furthermore, the softmax kernel can be expressed as a function of $K_\text{gauss}$: \begin{equation}\label{SM} \begin{split} \operatorname{SM}(\mathbf{q},\mathbf{k})=\exp\left(\frac{\lVert\mathbf{q}\rVert^2}{2}\right)K_\text{gauss}(\mathbf{q},\mathbf{k})\exp\left(\frac{\lVert\mathbf{k}\rVert^2}{2}\right) \end{split}\end{equation} Thus, the softmax kernel can be defined using $\phi$ where $h(\mathbf{x )=\exp\left(\frac{\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 {2}\right)$, $f_1=\sin$, and $f_2=\cos$.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Unfortunately, this representation behaves poorly around zero when the trigonometric functions return negative values, so Choromanski et al. introduce positive random features (PRFs) for softmax.
elements.sent-2411-136: This results in the form: \begin{equation}\label{PSM} \begin{split} \operatorname{SM}(\mathbf{q},\mathbf{k})=\mathbb{E}_{\omega\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\mathbf{I}_d)}\left[e^{\omega^\intercal\mathbf{q}-\frac{\lVert\mathbf{q}\rVert^2}{2}}e^{\omega^\intercal\mathbf{k}-\frac{\lVert\mathbf{k}\rVert^2}{2}}\right] \end{split}\end{equation} which implies $h(\mathbf{x )=\exp\left(-\frac{\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 {2}\right)$ and $f_1=\exp$.}}
elements.sent-2411-137: This approximation is further improved by forcing the $\omega$ random vectors to be normalized and orthogonal (OR+).
elements.sent-2411-138: Mapping the query and key matrices through these random projections, $\phi(\mathbf{Q )=\mathbf{Q ^\prime\in\mathbb{R ^{L\times r}$ and $\phi(\mathbf{K )=\mathbf{K ^\prime\in\mathbb{R ^{L\times r}$, attention can be reexpressed as follows: \begin{equation}\label{AATTN} \begin{split} \widehat{\operatorname{Attention}}(\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}) =\hat{\mathbf{D}}^{-1}(\underbrace{\mathbf{Q}^\prime((\mathbf{K}^\prime)^\intercal\mathbf{V})}_{\mathcal{O}(Lrd)}), \hat{\mathbf{D}} =\operatorname{diag}(\mathbf{Q}^\prime((\mathbf{K}^\prime)^\intercal\mathbf{1}_L)). \end{split}\end{equation} Thus, by carefully controlling the order of matrix multiplications, space complexity can be reduced from $\mathcal{O (L^2+Ld)$ to $\mathcal{O (Lr + Ld + rd)$ and time complexity can be reduced from $\mathcal{O (L^2d)$ to $\mathcal{O (Lrd)$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: We demonstrate the impact of the KRBlock and fast attention on runtime and memory in Tables \ref{test-scale and \ref{context-scale .}}
elements.sent-2411-140: In order to evaluate the performance of TNP-KR, we test its performance across standard benchmarks, which include GPs, image completion, and simple Bayesian Optimization (BO).
elements.sent-2411-141: All experiments were performed on a single 24GB Nvidia RTX 4090 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-142: For one-dimensional GPs, we evaluate the principal NP models on the the RBF, periodic, and Matérn 3/2 kernels.
elements.sent-2411-143: For each of these kernels, $\sigma^2$ can be factored out and used to standardize the data.
elements.sent-2411-144: Accordingly, in our tests, we assume the data has been standardized so the models can focus on learning and differentiating lengthscales.
elements.sent-2411-145: While standard benchmarks sample lengthscales from 0.1 to 0.6 uniformly to model over domain on the domain $[-2, 2]$, we sample lengthscales according to $\ell\sim\text{Beta (\alpha=3,\beta=7)$, which has both a mean and median of approximately 0.3.}
elements.sent-2411-146: This is a more challenging benchmark that allows for greater differentiation among models since more than 50\% of lengthscales fall below 0.3 and less than 10\% lie above 0.5.
elements.sent-2411-147: For the periodic kernel, we also sample the period uniformly from 0.5 to 2, which represents between 2 and 8 cycles on the domain $[-2, 2]$.
elements.sent-2411-148: Each model is seeded 5 times and trained on 100,000 batches of size 32.
elements.sent-2411-149: For each seed, the models are evaluated on a final test set consisting of 5,000 batches of size 32.
elements.sent-2411-150: Each sample in each batch consists of 50 randomly sampled locations and 100 linearly spaced points throughout the domain.
elements.sent-2411-151: Between 3 and 50 of the randomly sampled points are used as context points, and the test points consist of all 150 points.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \label{tx_enc} \caption{Transformer Encoder Block forward pass.} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Input: $x_0$, mask, $p_\text{dropout}$ \STATE $x_1 \gets \operatorname{MultiheadAttention}(x,x,x, \text{mask})$ \STATE $x_2 \gets x_0 + \operatorname{Dropout}(x_1, p_\text{dropout})$ \STATE $x_3 \gets \operatorname{LayerNorm}(x_2)$ \STATE $x_4 \gets \operatorname{FeedForward}(x_3)$ \STATE $x_5 \gets x_3 + \operatorname{Dropout}(x_4, p_\text{dropout})$ \RETURN $x_5$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{KRBlock forward pass.} \label{kr_blk} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: $qs_0$, $ks_0$, mask, $p_\text{dropout}$ \STATE $qs_1 \gets \operatorname{LayerNorm}_1(qs_0)$ \STATE $ks_1 \gets \operatorname{LayerNorm}_1(ks_0)$ \STATE $qs_2 \gets \operatorname{MultiheadAttention}(qs_1,ks_1, ks_1, \text{mask})$ \STATE $ks_2 \gets \operatorname{MultiheadAttention}(ks_1,ks_1, ks_1, \text{mask})$ \STATE $qs_3 \gets qs_0 + \operatorname{Dropout}(qs_2, p_\text{dropout})$ \STATE $ks_3 \gets ks_0 + \operatorname{Dropout}(ks_2, p_\text{dropout})$ \STATE $qs_4 \gets \operatorname{LayerNorm}_2(qs_3)$ \STATE $ks_4 \gets \operatorname{LayerNorm}_2(ks_3)$ \STATE $qs_5 \gets \operatorname{FeedForward}(qs_4)$ \STATE $ks_5 \gets \operatorname{FeedForward}(ks_4)$ \STATE $qs_6 \gets qs_3 + \operatorname{Dropout}(qs_5, p_\text{dropout})$ \STATE $ks_6 \gets ks_3 + \operatorname{Dropout}(ks_5, p_\text{dropout})$ \RETURN $qs_6, ks_6$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-177: Neural Processes (NPs) are a rapidly evolving class of models designed to directly model the posterior predictive distribution of stochastic processes.
elements.sent-2411-178: Originally developed as a scalable alternative to Gaussian Processes (GPs), which are limited by $\mathcal{O (n^3)$ runtime complexity, the most accurate modern NPs can often rival GPs but still suffer from an $\mathcal{O (n^2)$ bottleneck due to their attention mechanism.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: We introduce the Transformer Neural Process - Kernel Regression (TNP-KR), a scalable NP featuring: (1) a Kernel Regression Block (KRBlock), a simple, extensible, and parameter efficient transformer block with complexity $\mathcal{O (n_c^2 + n_c n_t)$, where $n_c$ and $n_t$ are the number of context and test points, respectively; (2) a kernel-based attention bias; and (3) two novel attention mechanisms: scan attention (SA), a memory-efficient scan-based attention that when paired with a kernel-based bias can make TNP-KR translation invariant, and deep kernel attention (DKA), a Performer-style attention that implicitly incoporates a distance bias and further reduces complexity to $\mathcal{O (n_c)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-180: These enhancements enable both TNP-KR variants to perform inference with 100K context points on over 1M test points in under a minute on a single 24GB GPU.
elements.sent-2411-181: On benchmarks spanning meta regression, Bayesian optimization, image completion, and epidemiology, TNP-KR with DKA outperforms its Performer counterpart on nearly every benchmark, while TNP-KR with SA achieves state-of-the-art results.
elements.sent-2411-182: While early Neural Processes (NPs) \cite{np, cnp focused primarily on the posterior predictive of Gaussian Processes (GPs) and simple data distributions like MNIST, many modern NPs tackle far more complex and data hungry distributions spanning ecology, epidemiology, climate, and population genetics.}
elements.sent-2411-183: Furthermore, many of these tasks require synthesizing on and off-grid data.
elements.sent-2411-184: Indeed, one of the most recent foundation models for climate, Aardvark \cite{aardvark , is a NP that synthesizes temperature, pressure, wind, humidity, and precipitation from on and off-grid data to generate forecasts that outperform traditional numerical weather prediction systems at a fraction of the computational cost.}
elements.sent-2411-185: As NPs grow in scope, there are two competing pressures: scale and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-186: Scale is particularly salient for applications involving high resolution sensor data, e.g. satellite imagery or 3D point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-187: On the other hand, many of these predictions need to be locally accurate to be actionable, e.g. city-level disaster preparedness.
elements.sent-2411-188: In order to make NPs as useful as possible, they should also be simple, extensible, and computationally tractable for researchers with limited computational budgets.
elements.sent-2411-189: Accordingly, we introduce TNP-KR, which we believe balances these desiderata well.. Our contributions include:.
elements.sent-2411-190: The Kernel Regression Block (KRBlock): a simple, stackable transformer block with $\mathcal{O (n_c^2 + n_cn_t)$ complexity for NPs that use full attention.}
elements.sent-2411-191: It is parameter efficient, sharing weights for query and key updates, and easily extensible, supporting spatial, temporal, and any other bias derived from the index set.
elements.sent-2411-192: A kernel-based attention bias: this allows our model to take advantage of both the precision of transformers and the local spatial bias of CNNs.
elements.sent-2411-193: This also enables TNP-KR with Scan Attention (SA) to be fully translation invariant.
elements.sent-2411-194: This bias improves performance across the board for both attention variants.
elements.sent-2411-195: Two new efficient attention mechanisms: (1) Scan Attention (SA) and (2) Deep Kernel Attention (DKA).
elements.sent-2411-196: SA implements full attention with a kernel bias using constant memory.
elements.sent-2411-197: DKA is a simple, approximate distance-biased attention mechanism that when paired with the KRBlock further reduces complexity to $\mathcal{O (n_c)$.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Neural processes (NPs) are a rapidly evolving class of models designed to directly model the posterior predictive distribution of stochastic processes.
elements.sent-2411-199: NPs are considered ``meta-learners'' because they do not model a specific function $h:\mathbf{s}\to\mathbf{f}$, where $\mathbf{s}$ is the index set and $\mathbf{f}$ are function values, but rather take as input context points $(\mathbf{s _c,\mathbf{f _c)$ and return a distribution over functions $g:(\mathbf{s _c,\mathbf{f _c)\to P_{h\mid\mathbf{s _c,\mathbf{f _c (h:\mathbf{s _t\to\mathbf{f _t)$, which can be evaluated at test points $\mathbf{s _t$ without retraining the model.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: In other words, a NP is a model that takes context points and returns a distribution over regression or classification functions.
elements.sent-2411-201: In general, NPs fall into two classes: conditional neural processes and latent neural processes.
elements.sent-2411-202: Conditional NPs deterministically encode observed or ``context'' points into a fixed representation, $\mathbf{r _c=f_\text{enc (\mathbf{s _c,\mathbf{f _c)$, and decode that fixed representation at test locations to retrieve functional output parameters, $\theta_t=f_\text{dec (\mathbf{r _c, \mathbf{s _t)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-203: For regression, it is common to set $\theta_t^{(i) =(\mathbf{f _\mu^{(i) ,\mathbf{f _\sigma^{(i) )$ for location $\mathbf{s _t^{(i)}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: For classification it is common to set $\theta_t^{(i) =[p_1^{(i) ,p_2^{(i) ,\ldots,p_k^{(i) ]$ where $p_k$ is the probability of class $k$ for location $\mathbf{s _t^{(i)}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-205: Conditional neural processes assume the test points factorize conditional on the fixed representation $\mathbf{r _c$, i.e. $p(\mathbf{f _t\mid \mathbf{s _c,\mathbf{f _c,\mathbf{s _t)=\prod_i p(\mathbf{f _t^{(i)}\mid\mathbf{s _t^{(i) ,\mathbf{r _c)$, and are trained to maximize the log likelihood of the data under the predicted distribution.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Latent neural processes introduce a latent variable $\psi_c$, which parameterizes a latent distribution that is sampled and then decoded, i.e. $\psi_c=f_\text{enc (\mathbf{s _c,\mathbf{f _c)$, $\mathbf{z _c\sim q(\mathbf{z}\mid \psi_c)$, and $\theta_t=f_\text{dec (\mathbf{z _c,\mathbf{s _t)$, theoretically enabling them to better encode global stochastic behavior \cite{np .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-207: Latent neural processes optimize an evidence lower bound (ELBO).
elements.sent-2411-208: Most transformers consist of an embedding layer, several transformer blocks, and a prediction head.
elements.sent-2411-209: Transformer blocks typically consist of multiheaded attention followed by a feedforward network, interspersed with residual connections.
elements.sent-2411-210: The attention mechanism \cite{attention was inspired by information retrieval and projects its input into three different matrices corresponding to queries, keys, and values.}
elements.sent-2411-211: The queries are matched against keys using a kernel, $\mathcal{K}$, and the resulting scores are used as weights for combining the associated values.
elements.sent-2411-212: The most common attention kernel is the dot-product softmax kernel, i.e. $\mathcal{K (\mathbf{Q ,\mathbf{K ,\mathbf{V )\coloneqq\text{softmax (\mathbf{QK ^\intercal/\sqrt{d_k )\mathbf{V}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Transformers can also be understood as permutation equivariant set functions.
elements.sent-2411-214: Conventional attention mechanisms have a space and time complexity of $\mathcal{O (n^2)$, which presents a challenge as the number of tokens increases.}
elements.sent-2411-215: There are 5 broad categories of research that attempt to address this: (1) sparsity \cite{sparse,longformer,bigbird,reformer,sinkhorn , (2) inducing points \cite{perceiver,settransformer , (3) low rank approximations \cite{linformer,performer,efficient , (4) reuse or sharing \cite{lazyformer,reusetransformer , and (5) memory and/or hardware optimized code \cite{memoryefficientattention,flash1,flash2,flash3 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: Most contemporary NPs, such as Latent Bottlenecked Attentive Neural Processes (LBANP) \cite{lbanp , Memory Efficient Neural Processes via Constant Memory Attention Blocks \cite{cmab , (Pseudo Token) Translation Equivariant Transformer Neural Processes (PT-TE-TNP)\cite{pttnp , and Gridded Transformer Neural Processes \cite{gridtnp , focus on (2) inducing points and use some form of Perceiver or Set Transformer attention \cite{perceiver,settransformer .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: Perciever and Set Transformer attention both introduce a number of latent (inducing) tokens, $k$, which is a fixed hyperparameter often much smaller than either the number of context points, $n_c$, or test points, $n_t$.
elements.sent-2411-218: Perceiver attention alternates between cross attention from context to latent tokens and self attention among the latent tokens.
elements.sent-2411-219: Set Transformer attention alternates between cross attention from context to latent tokens and cross attention from latent to context tokens.
elements.sent-2411-220: Both methods output latent tokens that can be cross attended to by the test points.
elements.sent-2411-221: NPs based on either attention mechanism have $\mathcal{O (k(n_c+n_t))$ complexity, assuming $k\ll n$.}
elements.sent-2411-222: While these methods can be far more computationally efficient, their performance is often bounded above by models like the Convolutional Conditional Neural Process (ConvCNP) \cite{convcnp and the Transformer Neural Process - Diagonal (TNP-D) \cite{tnp that directly use all observed points.}}
elements.sent-2411-223: In contrast to these methods, we focus on (3) low rank approximations (see \ref{subsec:performer ~and~\ref{subsec:deep_kernel_attention ) and (5) optimized memory efficient implementations (see \ref{subsec:scan_attention ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: Performer attention \cite{performer is a low rank attention mechanism based on an algorithm called Fast Attention Via Orthogonal Random features (FAVOR+).}
elements.sent-2411-225: It is nearly unbiased and offers uniform convergence and low variance.
elements.sent-2411-226: With $L$ denoting the sequence length, FAVOR+ constructs an attention matrix $\mathbf{A ^{L\times L}$ where $\mathbf{A (i,j)=K(\mathbf{q _i,\mathbf{k _j)$ without ever fully materializing it.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-227: By mapping the query and key matrices through positive orthogonal random projections, $\phi(\mathbf{Q )=\mathbf{Q ^\prime\in\mathbb{R ^{L\times r}$ and $\phi(\mathbf{K )=\mathbf{K ^\prime\in\mathbb{R ^{L\times r}$, attention can be re-expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\label{AATTN} \begin{split} \widehat{\operatorname{Attention}}(\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}) =\hat{\mathbf{D}}^{-1}(\underbrace{\mathbf{Q}^\prime((\mathbf{K}^\prime)^\intercal\mathbf{V})}_{\mathcal{O}(Lrd)}), \hat{\mathbf{D}} =\operatorname{diag}(\mathbf{Q}^\prime((\mathbf{K}^\prime)^\intercal\mathbf{1}_L)). \end{split}\end{equation} Thus, by carefully controlling the order of matrix multiplications, space complexity can be reduced from $\mathcal{O (L^2+Ld)$ to $\mathcal{O (Lr + Ld + rd)$ and time complexity can be reduced from $\mathcal{O (L^2d)$ to $\mathcal{O (Lrd)$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-228: Performer attention inspired our new attention variant, Deep Kernel Attention (DKA).
elements.sent-2411-229: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to incorporate a form of distance bias into a low-rank attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-230: For the purposes of NPs, data distributions can be classified as either translation invariant or not.
elements.sent-2411-231: Mathematically, for translation $T$ and function $f$, $f$ is translation invariant if $f(T(x))=f(x)$ for all $x$.
elements.sent-2411-232: Intuitively, this means that shifting the input does not change the output of the function.
elements.sent-2411-233: Many stationary stochastic processes, including stationary Gaussian Processes (GPs), are translation invariant in distribution.
elements.sent-2411-234: This property is commonly extended to phenomena that can be modeled using those stochastic processes, e.g. weather.
elements.sent-2411-235: On the other hand, natural images are not translation invariant because they have finite boundaries and non-stationary statistics, e.g. eyes are always located in a particular region of the image or the sky is always in the upper part of an image.
elements.sent-2411-236: Translation invariance is often a desirable property for a model because it allows it to extrapolate beyond the range of data it was trained on.
elements.sent-2411-237: We show in \ref{subsec:scan_attention that TNP-KR with SA can be translation invariant.}
elements.sent-2411-238: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), and specifically, graph transformers, leverage, attention bias to encode graph topology at various scales.
elements.sent-2411-239: For example, the Graphormer \cite{graphormer encodes spatial and structural biases using the shortest path and node centrality statistics.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Similarly, in large language modeling (LLM), Press et al. introduced Attention with Linear Biases (ALiBi) \yrcite{alibi , which replaces postitional embeddings with a bias that is proportional to the distance between tokens, i.e. $b(i,j)=-m\cdot|i-j|$ where $m$ is a fixed or learnable scalar and $i$ and $j$ are token positions.}
elements.sent-2411-241: ALiBi matched the performance of sinsusoidal embeddings while allowing the model to extrapolate far beyond its training range with almost no degredation in performance.
elements.sent-2411-242: Additionally, Wennberg et al. \yrcite{tisa use a more complex but more expressive RBF network to create translation invariant (self)-attention (TISA) bias: $f_\theta(k)=\sum_{s=1 ^S a_s\exp(-|b_s|(k-c_s)^2)$ where $a_s$, $b_s$, and $c_s$ are learnable for each basis function $s$.}}
elements.sent-2411-243: We leverage a similar attention bias in our TNP-KR models.
elements.sent-2411-244: The Transformer Neural Process - Kernel Regression (TNP-KR) architecture consists of an embedding layer, a stack of KRBlocks, and a prediction head.
elements.sent-2411-245: There are three components to the embedding: observation status ($\mathbf{obs}$), location or index ($\mathbf{s )$, and function value ($\mathbf{f )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-246: Observation status refers to whether a token is observed or unobserved, i.e. context or test, and this allows the shared parameters in the model to differentially update those tokens.
elements.sent-2411-247: For test points, the location is known but the function value is not, so $\mathbf{f _t$ is initialized to zeros.}
elements.sent-2411-248: After these components are independently embedded, they are concatenated and run through a co-embedding network. \begin{equation}\label{embed} \begin{split} \mathbf{e}_\text{obs}^{(i)} = \text{embed}_\text{obs}(\mathbf{obs}^{(i)}) \mathbf{e}_s^{(i)} = \text{embed}_s(\mathbf{s}^{(i)}) \mathbf{e}_f^{(i)} = \text{embed}_f(\mathbf{f}^{(i)}) \mathbf{e}^{(i)} = \text{embed}_\text{all}([\mathbf{e}_\text{obs}^{(i)}, \mathbf{e}_s^{(i)}, \mathbf{e}_f^{(i)}]) \end{split}\end{equation} Once the embeddings for context and test points are created, they are passed into a stack of KRBlocks as keys and queries, respectively.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\label{embed}  e_obs^{(i)} = embed_obs(obs^{(i)}) e_s^{(i)} = embed_s(s^{(i)}) e_f^{(i)} = embed_f(f^{(i)}) e^{(i)} = embed_all([e_obs^{(i)}, e_s^{(i)}, e_f^{(i)}]) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-249: A Kernel Regression Block (KRBlock) is a generic transformer block inspired by Nadaraya-Watson kernel regression \cite{nw_regression .}
elements.sent-2411-250: In the context of NPs, the cross attention from test to context points can be viewed as a form of Nadaraya-Watson kernel regression.
elements.sent-2411-251: With kernel $\mathcal{K}$, query $\mathbf{q _i\in\mathbb{R ^{D_{qk }$ (query projection of a test point), key $\mathbf{k _j\in\mathbb{R ^{D_{qk }$ (key projection of a context point), and value $\mathbf{v _j\in\mathbb{R ^{D_v}$ (value projection of a context point), we have: \begin{equation}\label{nadaraya-watson} \begin{split} \mathbf{v}_i^\prime=f(\mathbf{q}_i;\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}) =\sum_j \frac{\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{q}_i,\mathbf{k}_j)}{\sum_m \mathcal{K}(\mathbf{q}_i,\mathbf{k}_m)}\mathbf{v}_j \end{split}\end{equation} Stacking KRBlocks allows the model to perform iterative kernel regression on increasingly complex internal representations of queries (test points) and keys (context points).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-252: The KRBlock is similar to the architecture used in Efficient Queries Transformer Neural Processes (EQTNPs) \cite{eqtnp , but adds shared weights, pre-normalization, and meta-information.}
elements.sent-2411-253: Because of the observed/unobserved embedding in tokens, the KRBlock is able to share attention and feedforward parameters when updating queries and keys.
elements.sent-2411-254: Pre-normalized residual connections further improve performance and decrease sensitivity to learning rate schedules \cite{prenorm .}
elements.sent-2411-255: Finally, optional meta-information, $qs_meta$ and $ks_meta$, can be used to condition, bias, or extend the attention calculation, allowing a broader class of attention kernels, $\mathcal{K}$.
elements.sent-2411-256: For TNP-KR with kernel-based bias, meta-information corresponds to the index sets for context and test locations, that is, $qs_meta=\mathbf{s _t$ and $ks_meta=\mathbf{s _c$.}}
elements.sent-2411-257: The complexity for a KRBlock with standard attention is $\mathcal{O (n_c^2 + n_cn_t)$.}
elements.sent-2411-258: An architecture diagram is provided in \autoref{fig:kr_block_arch and the pseudocode is presented in Algorithm \autoref{alg:kr_block_algo .}}
elements.sent-2411-259: The time and space complexity for all models is in \autoref{tab:complexity .}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{KRBlock forward pass.} \label{alg:kr_block_algo} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: $qs_0$, $ks_0$, $\text{mask}_\mathbf{ks}$, $p_\text{dropout}$ \STATE Optional Input: $qs_meta$, $ks_meta$ \STATE $qs_1,\mathbf{ks}_1 \gets \operatorname{LayerNorm}(qs_0), \operatorname{LayerNorm}(ks_0)$ \STATE $qs_2 \gets \operatorname{MHA}_\theta(qs_1,ks_1, ks_1, \text{mask}_ks, qs_\mathbf{meta}, ks_\mathbf{meta})$ \STATE $ks_2 \gets \operatorname{MHA}_\theta(ks_1,ks_1, ks_1, \text{mask}_ks, ks_\mathbf{meta}, ks_\mathbf{meta})$ \STATE $qs_3 \gets qs_0 + \operatorname{Dropout}(qs_2, p_\text{dropout})$ \STATE $ks_3 \gets ks_0 + \operatorname{Dropout}(ks_2, p_\text{dropout})$ \STATE $qs_4,\mathbf{ks}_4 \gets \operatorname{LayerNorm}(qs_3), \operatorname{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{ks}_3)$ \STATE $qs_5 \gets \operatorname{FFN}_\psi(qs_4)$ \STATE $ks_5 \gets \operatorname{FFN}_\psi(ks_4)$ \STATE $qs_6 \gets qs_3 + \operatorname{Dropout}(qs_5, p_\text{dropout})$ \STATE $ks_6 \gets ks_3 + \operatorname{Dropout}(ks_5, p_\text{dropout})$ \STATE Return: $qs_6, ks_6$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-260: The success of Graphormer in GNNs \cite{graphormer and ALiBi \cite{alibi in LLMs has demonstrated that attention bias can be a useful inductive bias for transformer-based models.}}
elements.sent-2411-261: We define a kernel-based bias as any linear combination of reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) kernels, e.g. linear, exponential, periodic, Matérn, or RBF, or their products.
elements.sent-2411-262: For example, a NP designed to model climate change might use a bias that is the product of a linear and periodic kernel, $\mathcal{K =\mathcal{K _\text{linear (\mathbf{s , \mathbf{s ^\prime)\mathcal{K _\text{periodic (\mathbf{s , \mathbf{s ^\prime)$, to model cyclical but gradually increasing global temperatures.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-263: On the other hand a distance-based kernel like the RBF, $\mathcal{K _\text{RBF =\sigma^2\exp\left(-\frac{d^2 {2\ell}\right)$, may be more appropriate for epidemiological applications since transmission rates are generally an inverse function of distance.}}}
elements.sent-2411-264: Combining the softmax (SM) attention kernel, the kernel-based bias, and a learnable vector of weights, $\alpha$, the general form of attention used by TNP-KR models is: \begin{equation}\label{tnp_kr_attention} \begin{split} \mathcal{K}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}, \mathbf{s}_q, \mathbf{s}_k) =\text{SM}(\mathbf{q}^\intercal\mathbf{k}/\sqrt{d_k} + \sum_i \alpha_i\mathcal{K}_i(\mathbf{s}_q, \mathbf{s}_k)) \end{split}\end{equation} A particularly important property of this formulation is that when the embeddings for queries (test points) and keys (context points) do not include the index set, that is $\mathbf{s ^{(i)}$ is not used in calculating the token embedding $\mathbf{e ^{(i)}$, and the kernels used in the bias term are translation invariant, this attention mechanism becomes completely translation invariant.}}
elements.sent-2411-265: Moreover, different kernels can act on different components of the index set.
elements.sent-2411-266: For example, if the index set consists of a spatial location and time, $\mathbf{s =[x, t]$, one kernel could operate over the spatial dimension and another over the temporal dimension, $\mathcal{K (\mathbf{q _s, \mathbf{k _s)=\alpha_x\mathcal{K _x(\mathbf{s _{q (x), \mathbf{s _{k (x)) + \alpha_t\mathcal{K _t(\mathbf{s _{q (t), \mathbf{s _{q (t))$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-267: Again, if the aforementioned conditions are satisfied, this technique then becomes translation invariant in both space and time.
elements.sent-2411-268: A final added benefit is that, while not a proper RKHS kernel, $\mathcal{K _t$ could be modified as follows to enforce temporal causality without a masking function: \begin{equation}\label{tnp_kr_temporal} \begin{split} \mathcal{K}_t(\mathbf{s}_{q}(t), \mathbf{s}_{k}(t)) =\begin{cases} \mathcal{K}_t(\mathbf{s}_{q}(t), \mathbf{s}_{k}(t)) \text{if } \mathbf{s}_{k}(t) \le \mathbf{s}_{q}(t), -\infty \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{split}\end{equation} Temporal bias and causality is not explored in this paper and left for future research.}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\label{tnp_kr_temporal}  K_t(s_{q}(t), s_{k}(t)) = K_t(s_{q}(t), s_{k}(t)) if  s_{k}(t) \le s_{q}(t), -\infty otherwise  \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-269: The fundamental challenge with models that use full attention is the $\mathcal{O (n^2)$ time and space complexity, which is exacerbated by models that use attention bias since they require a second matrix of the same complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-270: Techniques such as memory efficient attention \cite{memory_efficient_attention for TPUs and Flash Attention 1, 2, and 3 \cite{flash1,flash2,flash3 for CUDA devices have largely solved memory complexity for standard attention, but do not support arbitrary bias functions, which means a fully materialized bias matrix must be passed to these methods, undermining their memory efficiency.}}
elements.sent-2411-271: Accordingly, we introduce Scan Attention (SA), a scan-based memory efficient attention mechanism that supports arbitrary bias functions.
elements.sent-2411-272: We implement SA in JAX \cite{jax , largely following the chunking algorithm defined in Flash Attention 2 \cite{flash2 , but adopting the scan-based tiling and gradient checkpointing of memory efficient attention.}}
elements.sent-2411-273: By using jax.lax.scan with gradient checkpointing, SA can calculate attention scores and custom bias terms on the fly with constant memory.
elements.sent-2411-274: The only requirements of SA, like its predecessors, are keeping track of the maximum attention score, $m(x)$, the normalization constant, $\ell(x)$, and the unnormalized output, $\mathbf{\tilde{O }$.}
elements.sent-2411-276: Then for the first block \autoref{eqn:sa_2 is computed, and for subsequent blocks, $j>1$, \autoref{eqn:sa_3 is computed. \begin{equation}\label{eqn:sa_1} \begin{split} x_i^{(j)} = \mathbf{q}^\intercal\mathbf{k}_i + \sum_m \alpha_m\mathcal{K}_m(\mathbf{s}_q, \mathbf{s}_{k_i}) f(x)^{(j)} = \left[e^{x_1^{(j)}-m(x)^{(j)}},\ldots, e^{x_B^{(j)}-m(x)^{(j)}}\right] \end{split}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eqn:sa_2} \begin{split} m(x)^{(1)} = \operatorname{rowmax}(x^{(1)}) \ell(x)^{(1)} = \operatorname{rowsum}(f(x)^{(1)}) \mathbf{\tilde{O}}^{(1)} =f(x)^{(1)}\mathbf{V}^{(1)} \end{split}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eqn:sa_3} \begin{split} m(x)^{(j:j>1)} = \max\left(m(x)^{(j-1)}, \operatorname{rowmax}(x^{(j)})\right) k^{(j:j>1)} = e^{m(x)^{(j-1)}-m(x)^{(j)}} \ell(x)^{(j:j>1)} = k^{(j)}\ell(x)^{(j-1)}+\operatorname{rowsum}(f(x)^{(j)}) \mathbf{\tilde{O}}^{(j:j>1)} =k^{(j)}\mathbf{\tilde{O}}^{(j-1)} + f(x)^{(j)}\mathbf{V}^{(j)} \end{split}\end{equation} In short, as each block of size $B$ is processed, three updates occur: (1) the maximum score, $m(x)^{(j)}$, is updated, (2) the normalization constants, $\ell(x)^{(j)}$, are rescaled and updated, and the (3) unnormalized output, $\mathbf{\tilde{O ^{(j)}$, is rescaled and updated.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-277: In the final step, the output is normalized by the final row sums, $\mathbf{O =\operatorname{diag (\ell(x)^{(n) )^{-1}\mathbf{\tilde{O ^{(n)}$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-313: The final benchmark is based on the Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) model, which models the spread of infectious outbreaks.
elements.sent-2411-314: It is governed by an infection rate, $\beta$, a recovery rate, $\gamma$, and the number of initial infections, $\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-315: For this benchmark, we sample $\beta\sim\operatorname{Beta (2, 8)$, $\gamma\sim\operatorname{InvGamma (5, 0.4)$, and $\omega\sim\operatorname{randint (1,5)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-316: In expectation, this refers to an infection rate of 20\% upon exposure and a 10-day recovery period (similar to COVID-19).
elements.sent-2411-317: Furthermore, the infection rate, $\beta$, is decreased as an inverse function of distance from the infected individual.
elements.sent-2411-318: We train all models on 64x64 images and use the same sampling rate regime as the other image benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-319: For this benchmark we also train TNP-KR: SA in a fully translation invariant fashion and show that it can be applied to images as large as 1024x1024 with a minimal decrease in performance.
elements.sent-2411-320: We include timing results in \autoref{tab:sir_scale_ms , which show that TNP-KR: SA can process a 1M+ pixel image in $\approx 50$ seconds, while the Performer and DKA variants can do it in $\approx 0.3$ milliseconds.}

elements.sent-2411-321: There have been roughly four waves of NP models: (1) an initial wave introducing the foundational NPs and CNPs \cite{np,cnp ; (2) a second wave introducing attention, bootstrapping, and translation equivariance in ANP, CANP, BNP, BANP, and ConvCNP \cite{anp,bnp,convcnp ; (3) a third wave migrating to transformers with TNP-D, TNP-ND, and TNP-A \cite{tnp ; and (4) a forth wave focused on latent pseudo tokens in LBANP, CMABNP, (PT-)TE-TNP, and Gridded TNPs \cite{lbanp,cmab,pttnp,gridtnp .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-322: We compare TNP-KR to variants in waves (1)-(3) because variants in wave (4) are typically bounded above by models like TNP-D and ConvCNP, which TNP-KR variants exceed both in terms of scalability and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-323: We also do not address autoregressive sampling, which has been demonstrated to consistently improve performance \cite{autoregressive_cnps for all NPs.}
elements.sent-2411-324: Finally, we do not consider parameterized covariance variants, e.g. TNP-ND (non-diagonal), because they are either outperformed by autoregressive variants or cause large increases in space and time complexity.
elements.sent-2411-325: In this work, we introduce TNP-KR, a simple, scalable, and extensible NP model with kernel-based attention bias.
elements.sent-2411-326: TNP-KR: SA achieves state-of-the-art performance and TNP-KR: DKA outperforms the Performer variant on almost every benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-327: We also demonstrate how TNP-KR: SA can be trained in a completely translation invariant fashion, allowing the model to be trained on small patches, e.g. 64x64, and tested on much larger surfaces, e.g. 1024x1024, with almost no degradation in performance.
elements.sent-2411-328: This paper presents work whose goal is to advance the field of Machine Learning.
elements.sent-2411-329: There are many potential societal consequences of our work, none which we feel must be specifically highlighted here.
